definition: The escape or way out from a conditioned entanglement, danger, or problematic framework.
notes: The project keeps escape as the default because it preserves the directional out-from force without collapsing nissaraṇa into the broader release family. Way out remains a controlled alternate when explanatory prose needs plainer English, especially in gratification-danger-escape triads. The OSF glossary supports keeping this directional way-out force explicit, and SN 22.26 supports the stable assāda-ādīnava-nissaraṇa triad in relation to the five clung-to heaps. In this repository, nissaraṇa differs from vimutti: vimutti is release, whereas nissaraṇa is the escape from something. It also differs from vossagga, which is relinquishment by letting go; from tarati and tiṇṇa, which preserve crossing imagery; and from yogakkhema, which names security from the yoke rather than the way out from a specific danger. Translators should therefore resist flattening nissaraṇa into generic freedom or liberation language, especially in assāda-ādīnava-nissaraṇa formulas where the triad's directional logic matters.

context_rules.context: In most doctrinal and formula contexts
In explanatory prose where the directional sense needs to stay very plain
context_rules.notes: Allowed alternate when the line benefits from it.

context_rules.context: In formulas of the form assāda, ādīnava, nissaraṇa
context_rules.notes: Keep the triad stable rather than rotating among release-language alternatives.

context_rules.context: When the text explicitly frames one as seeing the escape from a phenomenon
context_rules.notes: Let the out-from structure stay audible in English.

example_phrases.pali: pañcupādānakkhandhesu assādo ca ādīnavo ca nissaraṇaṃ ca
example_phrases.translation: the gratification, danger, and escape in relation to the five clung-to heaps
example_phrases.notes: Standard doctrinal triad showing nissaraṇa as the directional way out.

example_phrases.pali: vedanānaṃ assādo ca ādīnavo ca nissaraṇaṃ ca
example_phrases.translation: the gratification, danger, and escape in relation to felt experience
example_phrases.notes: Shows the same triad applying to the experience-process family.

authority_basis.scope: Supports keeping nissaraṇa distinct from broader release-language and preserving the directional way-out force.
Supports the stable gratification-danger-escape triad in relation to the five clung-to heaps.

translation_policy.default_scope: Use 'escape' as the default rendering in the entry's governed doctrinal and formula contexts unless a recorded context rule overrides it.
translation_policy.when_not_to_apply: Use 'way out' only when explanatory prose clearly benefits. Do not flatten nissaraṇa into generic freedom, liberation, or release language.
translation_policy.drift_risk: Prevents the directional escape-family from collapsing into generic liberation-language or into the distinct crossing and security families.
